---
title: "Electronics: From Elements to Systems"
description: "A free, practical guide from first measurements to complete electronic systems."
page-role: frontmatter
---

# Start with a voltage. End with a system.

Electronics is often taught as a pile of formulas to memorize and circuits to
recognize. This book takes a different position: electronics becomes
understandable when the many ways we describe a circuit stop disagreeing. The
physical assembly, the circuit diagram, the governing equations, a simulation,
the firmware, the measured waveform, the requirement, and the test that accepts
it are all descriptions of one object. When they agree, you understand the
circuit. When they disagree, one of them is wrong—and that disagreement is the
most useful information the bench can offer.

The book develops that agreement one layer at a time. It begins with safe
measurement and simple sources and loads. It then builds through passive
networks, semiconductor devices, analog and digital circuits, embedded software,
signals, control, power, communication, and product realization. The final part
uses complete systems to show why the earlier details matter. The path is long
on purpose: a single measured voltage and a field-qualified instrument are the
same craft practiced at different scales.

## What this book is

It is a free, practical, model-based introduction. *Practical* means results are
earned on a bench, not asserted: every important idea is meant to be predicted,
built, measured, and reconciled. *Model-based* means the goal is never a single
number but a model with stated assumptions and known limits—you should always be
able to say which approximation you are using and what would break it. In that
spirit it follows the bench-first tradition of practical electronics
[@horowitz2015art] while grounding device behavior in the quantitative circuit
models developed in the standard microelectronics literature
[@sedra2020microelectronic].

It is *not* a formula sheet, a cookbook of circuits to copy, or a substitute for
a datasheet, a standard, or supervised laboratory training. Where a claim rests
on device physics, metrology, or regulation, the book points to primary and
canonical sources rather than restating them from memory.

## The through-line: one system, many representations

A worked example in this book tries to connect several of these views of the
same system at once:

- physical construction and the energy path;
- schematic with named nodes and reference directions;
- equations and their assumptions;
- simulation or another executable model;
- measured data with its uncertainty;
- software, state, and timing;
- requirements and the tests that accept them.

Keeping these aligned is the recurring discipline of the whole text. It is why
SI units and explicit reference directions are used throughout
[@bipm2019si], why a negative result is read as "reality opposes my chosen
reference" rather than "error," and why measurement uncertainty is treated as a
first-class quantity to be estimated, not an afterthought [@jcgm2008gum]. The
[preface](preface.qmd) states the notation and evidence conventions in full.

## How the path is organized

The book is seven parts and a set of working appendices. Each part answers one
central question and hands a durable capability to the next.

| Stage | Central question |
|---|---|
| Foundations | What are voltage, current, resistance, energy, and a trustworthy measurement? |
| Devices and analog | How do physical devices shape and amplify real signals? |
| Digital hardware | How are values, decisions, state, and computation built from circuits? |
| Embedded systems | How does software interact with time, energy, peripherals, and failure? |
| System domains | How do we sample, control, power, and communicate? |
| Product realization | How does a design become reproducible, compliant, and maintainable hardware? |
| Complete systems | How are cross-domain trade-offs verified and handed to another person? |

The middle parts each carry a canonical reference alongside the chapter text:
signals and sampling follow the standard signals-and-systems treatment
[@oppenheim1996signals]; digital logic, memory, and processors build toward
computer architecture [@harris2021digital]; feedback and control draw on modern
feedback-systems theory [@astrom2021feedback]; and realization brings in
electromagnetic compatibility as a design constraint, not a final test
[@ott2009electromagnetic]. The mathematical and engineering-practice appendices
(M01–M03, T01–T03) are working references, used just in time when a chapter first
needs a technique—not a hurdle to clear before starting.

Each chapter can support self-study or classroom teaching. Course routes are
published separately and point to the same chapters; there are no
course-specific copies of the theory, and no chapter is forked for a particular
semester.

## Read, predict, test, decide

Do not read electronics as a list of formulas. Before a result is revealed,
predict its direction and approximate magnitude. State the model and its limits.
Then compare calculation, simulation, and measurement, and treat any
disagreement as information rather than noise. Finish by deciding what the
evidence actually permits you to claim—no stronger.

That cycle rides on an evidence ladder that runs from a rough estimate to a
qualified claim:

```text
estimate → calculation → simulation → measurement → repeated test
         → independent reproduction → field evidence → qualified claim
```

Not every question needs the top rung. The consequence of being wrong sets how
much evidence is appropriate: a sign convention can rest on a calculation, while
a safety or lifecycle claim cannot. Learning to choose the right rung is itself
part of the engineering.

## How to move through the book

The default route follows the sidebar from elements to systems, and it is the
safest route for a new reader because later parts assume the measurement habits,
reference directions, model limits, and evidence practices established in
Part I. Readers aiming at a specific destination—a bench investigation, an
analog instrument, an embedded system, a custom product, or a complete field
system—can use the [reading roadmap](roadmap.qmd) to emphasize a target while
still respecting prerequisites. The roadmap also explains chapter maturity and
maps the practice-and-project spine that turns reading into evidence.

::: {.callout-note title="Development edition"}
The table of contents and dependency structure are complete, but the chapters
are currently outlines. Their front matter reports maturity explicitly; an
outline is a scope commitment, not yet a teachable or safety-authorizing chapter.
:::

::: {.callout-warning title="Safety boundary"}
Early practical work is deliberately low voltage and current limited. Reading a
chapter is not authorization for mains, high-voltage, high-energy battery, RF
power, rotating-machine, medical, or safety-critical work. Use approved
facilities, supervision, and local procedures.
:::

Read the [preface](preface.qmd) next for the notation, evidence standard, and
practical expectations, then begin with
[safe practice](01-foundations/f01-safe-practice.qmd).
